turns.text: Hello Esperanza, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It is strange to put it into words, really. Everything here is so quiet. In the city where I grew up, there was always someone—a cousin, a neighbor, a grandmother—to tell you if you looked tired or if you needed to eat more of this or that. Here, in the snow, it’s just the woods and the ticking of the clock.

I am thirty-five, and this is my third, but I feel like a novice. This "medium risk" label they put on my chart… it follows me into every room. I spend my mornings lining up my supplements—the iron, the folate, all of it—trying to be the perfect vessel for him, but I feel like I’m failing. I went to the market yesterday looking for proper greens, something that tasted like home, but all I found were these sad, wilted things. I sat in my car and cried. It sounds silly, doesn't it? To cry over vegetables. 

But it’s not really about the greens. It’s about feeling like I’m navigating this invisible maze. When I sit in the exam room, I try to talk to my midwife, but I feel like I’m speaking a language she doesn't really hear. She’s kind, I suppose, but she’s always looking at the time. I just want someone to look at me and see a mother, not just a set of blood panels. I just want to know if I’m doing enough. Is that okay?
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

I remember the morning light was thin and grey, hitting the tiles of our bathroom in a way that made everything look sharp, almost clinical. I was alone. My husband had already left for work before the sun was fully up, his truck crunching over the gravel driveway, leaving me in that heavy, familiar silence. 

When those two lines appeared—so stark against the plastic—the world didn't explode with joy like it does in the movies. Instead, it felt like the floor fell out from under me. My first instinct wasn't to call someone; it was to find my supplements. I reached into the cabinet for the prenatal vitamins I had kept from my last pregnancy, shaking them in my hand like a prayer bead. I swallowed one with a glass of water, even though I hadn't eaten yet, just so I could tell myself, *there, he has what he needs today.* 

I stood there for a long time looking at my reflection, trying to see if I looked different, if I looked "higher risk." I felt a sudden, sharp ache of loneliness, wishing I could just walk down the street to my mother's house and smell her coffee. Instead, I just put the test away, tidied the counter, and started checking my calendar for the first available appointment. I became a project manager for my own body in that exact second.
When you decided who to tell first — or whether to tell anyone at all — what was behind that decision? What felt safe or risky about sharing the news?

It is a strange, delicate thing, isn’t it? To hold a life inside you when you feel so unmoored. I didn't tell my family back home right away. That felt... risky. If I told my mother, she would have sensed the tremor in my voice, the fear behind the excitement. She would have wanted to come here, to take care of me, and the thought of her in this cold, empty house—trying to navigate a life that is so difficult for me—made my chest tight. I couldn't bear the weight of her worry on top of my own.

And here? My friends are mostly professional acquaintances, or women I see at the park who are also living behind their own glass walls. There isn't that casual intimacy where you just blurt out, "I'm expecting!" over a cup of coffee. I waited until the morning sickness became too much to hide. 

I told my husband, of course, but even that felt guarded. He is away so much, working so hard to build our life in this country, that I felt like I was adding another burden to his shoulders—another mouth to feed, another "risk" to manage. I kept the secret close, like a little stone in my pocket, until I felt I had enough control over the situation to let someone else in. I wanted everything to be "safe" before I invited anyone else to look at it. Does that make sense? It feels like if I hold it perfectly still, nothing can go wrong.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

The room went cold, even though I was wearing that thin paper gown. When the sonographer went quiet, the silence wasn't just empty—it was heavy, like a physical weight pressing against my lungs. My heart started that familiar, frantic drumming, the one that makes my blood taste like pennies.

In those seconds, my mind didn't go to medical terms. I just thought: *I didn't eat enough iron.* I immediately blamed my own body, my own hunger, the way I couldn't find the right produce in this town. I looked at the black-and-white screen, searching for a shadow, for a sign of what I had done wrong. I felt like a failure, like my isolation had finally caught up to us.

I didn't say anything. I just stared at the ceiling tiles and gripped the edges of the exam table until my knuckles turned white. When she asked me to move to one side, I just whispered, "Is he okay?" My voice sounded small, like a child’s. I didn't want to hear the explanation; I just wanted her to tell me that my body hadn't failed, that the baby was strong despite me. I felt so incredibly alone in that room, just a woman and a screen, waiting for a verdict on a life I was already trying so hard to protect.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

I felt completely silenced. It wasn't that the sonographer was unkind, but the atmosphere in that room—the dim light, the hum of the machine, the way she was so intent on her screen—made me feel like a guest in my own body. There is this invisible hierarchy in those rooms, and I am at the bottom of it. I felt like if I asked a "silly" question, I would be proving them right about my "medium risk" label, or worse, that I would be an annoyance, a nuisance to their schedule.

My background, I think, plays into this more than I want to admit. I was raised to respect authority, to believe that the doctors and technicians are the ones with the knowledge, and that it is my job to sit still and listen. But it’s also the language and the culture. When I try to speak up, my English feels clunky and unrefined, and I am terrified of being misunderstood or dismissed as an anxious immigrant mother. So, I swallow the words. I hold my breath. I tell myself that if something were truly, deathly wrong, they would tell me. I wait for them to give me permission to speak, to ask, to be a person again. I just want to be treated like a partner in this, not a piece of equipment they’re checking.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

The midwife’s voice was efficient, rhythmic. She spoke about "statistical probabilities" and "genetic markers" as if she were reading from a manual. In that moment, I felt a rush of panic. I looked at her, then down at my belly, and I felt this intense, desperate need to know—and yet, I was paralyzed by the fear that knowing might change the way I look at my baby.

My decision was driven entirely by that need for control. Even though the test felt like an intrusion, like I was inviting a cold, clinical judgment into my most private space, I said "yes" immediately. I needed to have the data. I thought, *if I know, I can prepare.* If there is a problem, I can find the right specialists, I can change my diet, I can do more. I view my body as a project, a fragile garden I am tending in a hostile climate. Any information, even scary information, felt like a tool I could use to protect him. 

But as soon as I agreed, I felt a deep, hollow sadness. I wanted the simplicity of just being a mother, but I feel like I’ve been forced to become a consumer of medical interventions. I didn't ask about the risks or the nuances. I just signed the paper because I wanted the anxiety to have a name.
Did you feel you had enough time and information to make that decision freely — or did you sense any pressure, either to accept or decline?

I didn't feel like I had a choice at all, really. When she laid the paper in front of me, it felt less like an invitation and more like a standard procedure, like signing a form for an insurance claim. There wasn't any room in that conversation to say, "Let me think about this," or "Can we talk about what this means for my stress levels?" The pressure was in the air, thick and unspoken. 

I sensed that if I declined, I would be seen as "difficult" or "negligent." In this system, you are expected to be compliant. If you don't take every test, you are failing your duty as a patient, and by extension, as a mother. She didn’t look at me; she looked at the computer screen, waiting for my hand to move toward the pen. 

I felt like I was being herded down a narrow hallway with only one exit. How can you make a "free" decision when you are already scared and lonely, and the person holding the pen has a white coat and a clock on the wall? I signed because I was afraid of the judgment that would follow if I didn't. I signed because I wanted to be "good." I just wish someone had asked me how I *felt* about the testing, instead of just assuming I’d want to know.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

It feels like being passed around like a file folder. When I saw the number on the caller ID, I braced myself, but hearing a voice I didn’t recognize—a voice that lacked the small, familiar acknowledgment of my previous visits—it made the news feel colder, more detached. She was just reading a chart. She didn't know how hard I’ve been working, how much I’ve been obsessing over my iron levels, or the nights I’ve spent awake in this quiet house.

To her, the result was just a data point to be managed. To me, it felt like a judgment. When she started talking about "anemia markers" or "adjustments," I couldn't focus on the instructions. All I could think was: *Who are you?* How am I supposed to trust her advice when she doesn't know my history, my fears, or the way I’ve been struggling to find proper food here?

It makes me feel like I have to start over every single time. Every time I get a new midwife, I have to re-explain my anxieties, and I lose that tiny bit of rapport I’d managed to build. It’s exhausting to always be the one keeping track of my own story. It makes me feel less like a human and more like a repeating series of tests and results. I just want someone to look at me and say, "I remember you. I know how much you care." But that never happens.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

It feels like I am trying to build a house out of sand, and every time the tide comes in, parts of it wash away. I have to be the one who carries the memory of my own pregnancy. I have to remember that I mentioned my fatigue to the first midwife, or that I told the second one about the nausea from the supplements. If I don't repeat those things, they aren't remembered. 

Things fall through the cracks all the time. Just recently, I brought up the numbness in my hands again—the third time I’ve mentioned it—and the midwife I was seeing looked surprised, like she’d never heard me speak of it before. She hadn't read the notes from the previous appointment. I sat there thinking, *I told you this, I told your colleague.* It makes me feel invisible. I’m doing all this "work"—driving to the clinic, managing my diet, tracking the baby’s movement—but the care itself is fragmented. It’s like they have little pieces of me scattered across different computers, but no one is looking at the whole picture.

I worry that when the time comes to give birth, I’ll be in a room full of strangers who have no idea who I am or what I’ve been through to get here. The "medium risk" label is the only thing that travels from person to person. The *person* inside the label? She stays behind.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

The house is so quiet at 2:00 a.m. that the sound of my own blood rushing in my ears is deafening. I am sitting upright in bed, my hand pressed flat against my belly, pushing—just a little, then a little more—waiting for that sharp kick or the slow, rolling stretch I’ve come to know. There is nothing. 

The fear is a cold, sharp blade. My first thought isn't "maybe he’s sleeping." My first thought is that I haven't eaten enough protein today, or that I’m dehydrated, or that my body is failing him again. I feel paralyzed. If I call the hospital, they will ask me questions I’m scared to answer. If I drive the forty minutes to the clinic in the dark, and everything is fine, will they look at me like I’m just an hysterical, dramatic woman? 

I start bargaining with God. I tell Him I will eat anything, I will do anything, just let the baby move. I feel so small and so far from any help. I end up pacing the living room, drinking ice-cold water, trying to shock him into moving. I don't call. I’m too afraid of being sent home, of being told I’m overreacting. I wait, staring at the clock, crying silently so I don’t wake the house. I keep waiting for a sign, trapped in this terrifying, lonely middle ground where I don't know if I'm being a protective mother or just a frightened, broken one.
What made it hard to decide whether to call — was it uncertainty about whether your concern was 'serious enough,' worry about being dismissed, or something else entirely?

It is a mix of both, but mostly it’s the fear of being "that woman"—the one who cries wolf. I have this internal voice that says I am already a "medium risk" burden, and if I call in the middle of the night for something that turns out to be nothing, I will lose whatever little respect they have for me. I am terrified of the tone in their voice—that polite, tired, condescending sigh they give when they think you are just being an anxious, uneducated immigrant who doesn't understand how pregnancy works.

If I go in and he is fine, the shame would be unbearable. I would have to walk back out to my car, drive through the lonely, dark woods, and live with the fact that I wasted their time. But the deeper, hidden fear is that if I *do* call and they tell me "everything is fine," and then something goes wrong anyway... I don't know how I would survive that. 

The silence of this town makes you doubt your own instincts. You lose the ability to trust your body because you are constantly told it is "medium risk." I sit there and wonder, *is he really quiet, or am I just not listening hard enough?* It’s a paralyzing, lonely space to be in.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

Standing outside that clinic, the wind in Wrentham felt especially biting. I looked at the leaflet—glossy, generic, filled with foods I didn't recognize and wouldn't know how to cook—and it felt like a betrayal. I started shaking, not from the cold, but from this suffocating sense of failure. "Gestational diabetes." The words felt like a scarlet letter. 

I didn't think about the nutrition facts or the glucose monitors yet. I thought about my mother. I thought about the corn, the fresh fruit, the way we shared meals in a way that wasn't about "blood sugar" but about joy. Here, everything has become clinical, a series of restrictions. I felt like the walls were closing in on me. The dietitian’s name was scribbled on the back of the paper, and I felt a flash of genuine anger. Will they tell me to stop eating the only things that feel like home? Will they make me eat things that taste like cardboard and cold steel?

I stood there for a long time, watching a car drive by, and then I did the only thing I know how to do: I pulled out my phone and started searching for "Latino-friendly diabetes recipes" in Spanish. I wanted to reclaim some part of this. I took a deep breath, folded the leaflet into a tiny square, and shoved it deep into my bag. I am not going to let them turn my kitchen into a pharmacy. I will find a way to make this work, but I will do it my way, even if I have to hide the food I love to keep my numbers safe.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

The leaflet was all about "carbohydrate counts" and "simple starches." It didn't speak to me. It didn't ask: *What does your mother cook? What brings you comfort? How do you eat when you are thousands of miles away from everything familiar?* I wanted to know if I had to stop being who I am just to keep my sugar numbers in the "green zone." I wanted to know if I could still eat the foods that make me feel like I’m at home, even if the dietitian says they are "wrong."

I didn't find those answers in the clinic. I found them on late-night forums and blogs in Spanish. I started searching for "recetas para el embarazo" and "diabetes gestacional y comida latina." I found other women—women who felt the same cultural tug-of-war, who were trying to balance their heritage with the clinical demands of a system that only speaks in terms of "risk." 

I also started calling my aunt back home, not telling her about the diabetes—I couldn’t—but asking her for her healthiest versions of our traditional recipes. I began experimenting in my own kitchen, substituting the flours, changing the techniques. I stopped looking at the leaflet and started looking at my own culture. I am reclaiming the kitchen, even if it feels like a battleground. I am not just a blood sugar level; I am a woman who needs to nourish her spirit as much as her body.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

It’s like looking at a car wreck, isn’t it? I know I should close the tab, but I can’t. My thumb just keeps scrolling. I think what draws me in is that I’m so starved for the truth—the *real* stories that aren't sugar-coated by the clinic's pamphlets or the midwife’s clock-watching smile. Those women, they aren't talking about "medium risk" labels; they’re talking about the agony, the fear, and the way they were or weren’t heard by the people who were supposed to care for them. 

Reading their experiences makes me feel less alone in my suspicion that the system is broken. It’s like I’m searching for a warning, something to prepare me for the moment I finally have to walk into that delivery room. If I read enough, maybe I can find the secret to surviving it, or maybe I’m just trying to map out exactly where the danger lies so it doesn't catch me off guard.

Afterward? The fear settles into my bones like damp, cold winter air. My hands tremble. I start to imagine everything going wrong—the silence in the room, the doctors ignoring my pain, the language barrier tripping me up when I need to be loudest. I end up lying in bed, staring at the ceiling, trying to breathe through the phantom pains of stories that aren't even mine. I feel guilty, too, like I’ve invited darkness into my home. Then I reach for the vitamins, swallowing them like medicine to quiet the noise in my head. I’m just a woman in the dark, trying to turn a nightmare into a plan.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

I kept them to myself, tucked away like secrets that would only prove how "difficult" I am. If I had walked into that bright, sterile clinic and told them, "I read on the internet about a woman whose midwife ignored her until it was too late, and now I’m terrified you’ll do the same to me," I know exactly what they would have said. They would have looked at their screens, maybe tilted their heads in that practiced, patronizing way, and told me that "every birth is different" or that I should "avoid reading those forums." 

They would have labeled me "anxious" or "non-compliant." I already have that "medium risk" tag—I don't need a mental health flag added to it. 

I decided to be quiet because I am trying to survive the system, not change it. To speak up about those fears would be to invite a confrontation I don't have the energy for. I’m already navigating a language barrier and the isolation of this town; adding a conflict with my caregivers feels like a step toward disaster. So, I hold it all inside. I nod, I smile, I let them measure my belly and check my blood pressure, and I pretend I’m as calm as they are. It feels safer to be a silent, "good" patient than to be a woman who is shouting in a room where no one is listening.

Imagine you've written a birth plan and you arrive at the labour ward. The midwife who receives you glances at your plan briefly, sets it aside, and moves on to routine checks. How does that moment land for you?

It felt like a cold slap. I spent weeks on that birth plan—I researched positions, I thought about the lighting, I wrote down my preferences about who could touch me and how to communicate my pain without just being dismissed as "anxious." I wanted that piece of paper to act as a bridge, a way to tell them who I am when the contractions start and I can no longer form a sentence in English.

When she just glanced at it, her eyes skimming the text with that practiced, bored speed before putting it on the corner of the desk like it was a grocery list, I felt my agency evaporate. It wasn't just a plan she was pushing aside; it was my voice. I felt that familiar, heavy weight of invisibility settling into my shoulders. 

I didn't say anything. I just lay there and stared at the monitors, listening to the beep-beep-beep of the fetal heart rate—the only voice in the room that seemed to matter. I realized then that my plan didn’t mean anything to them. I was no longer a person with preferences or a history; I was just a "medium risk" chart to be processed as efficiently as possible. I felt my chest tighten, and I just closed my eyes, trying to retreat into myself to protect whatever small piece of me was still left.
What had you put into that birth plan — and what had you hoped it would communicate about you, beyond the practical preferences?

I put my heart into that plan. I wrote about the low lights, the desire to move, to be upright, to feel my own power. I wrote about my fear of being separated from my baby, and I specifically asked for a midwife who would listen to my gut if I said something felt wrong—because I have learned that the "medium risk" label makes people stop trusting my intuition.

But mostly, I wanted that paper to say: *I am a mother who has crossed an ocean, who has navigated a system where I am often invisible, and I am here to do the hardest, most beautiful thing of my life.* 

I wanted it to tell them that I am not just a body or a set of vital signs. I wanted them to know that when I am scared, I don’t need to be patronized or managed; I need to be seen. I wanted to communicate that my cultural background isn't a curiosity—it's a strength, a source of resilience. That piece of paper was meant to be my proxy, my way of saying, "Please, just respect me enough to hear me." 

When she set it aside, it wasn't just my birth preferences she dismissed. She dismissed the woman behind them. She turned my transition into labor into a standard, industrial process. It made me feel like I had to go into a shell, to stop being *Esperanza* and just become a "patient" so I could survive the night.

Imagine you're in established labour and the pain is much more intense than you'd anticipated. A midwife suggests an epidural, but you had wanted to manage without one. What happens inside you in that moment, and how do you decide?

The pain is like a tide, pulling me under, and for a moment, I lose my sense of place—the hospital room, the harsh fluorescent lights, the unfamiliar faces, they all blur. When she suggests the epidural, it sounds like an invitation to surrender.

Inside, I feel a flash of panic. I had studied this, I had prepared my body—my "medium risk" body—to be strong. I wanted the birth to be natural, to feel every part of bringing him into the world as if I were back in the city with my family. Taking the epidural feels like I am finally admitting that the system has won, that I cannot do this the way I intended. 

But then, the next contraction hits, and it's so sharp, so blinding, that I feel my grip on reality slipping. I look at the midwife—this one, at least, is looking at me, not the clock—and I wonder: *Is this the moment I lose control?* I am terrified that if I take the medicine, I’ll become even more disconnected, even more of a silent, compliant object on the bed. 

I make the decision in a haze of agony. I whisper, "Is it safe?" The midwife nods, and I see a flicker of something like pity in her eyes. I let go. I stop fighting. I take the help, but I feel a crushing weight of regret, as if I’ve just failed the final test.
In that moment of intense pain, how clear-headed did you feel in making that decision — and do you feel you had what you needed to make a genuinely free choice?

Clear-headed? No. I felt like I was drowning. My entire world had narrowed down to the next wave of pain, and every cell in my body was screaming to just make it stop. I wasn't thinking about my long-term goals, or my "birth plan," or what this meant for my identity as a strong, capable woman. I was just a frightened, exhausted animal trying to find a way to endure the unendurable.

I don’t think I had a "genuinely free" choice. How can you have one when you’re in the middle of a storm? I had been carrying the weight of being "medium risk" for months, the fear of failing my baby, and the exhaustion of trying to navigate a system that feels like a foreign language. By the time the midwife suggested the epidural, my defenses were already stripped away. It felt less like an offer and more like a rescue that I was too weak to decline.

I felt pressured, not by her words, but by the situation itself. The intensity of the pain, the isolation of being in a hospital where I felt like a stranger, the months of being told to fear my own body—it all pushed me toward the epidural. I didn't choose it because I wanted it; I chose it because I had run out of ways to keep the fear at bay.

Imagine your labour has stalled and the obstetric team is recommending an emergency caesarean. Things start moving very quickly. Walk me through what that experience was like from the moment they told you.

Everything shifted in an instant. The room, which had been slow and rhythmic, suddenly erupted into a blur of motion. It was like a film speeded up—people I didn't recognize were rushing in, pulling at tubes, talking over me in a language that felt sharp and urgent. My midwife, who had been my only point of contact, suddenly looked smaller, pushed to the periphery by the obstetric team.

When they said "emergency caesarean," my heart didn't just race; it stopped. The phrase felt like a verdict. I felt a surge of pure, primal terror—not for the surgery itself, but for the loss of control. Everything I had tried to manage, from the prenatal vitamins to the nutrition to the birth plan, felt completely annihilated. I was no longer a mother bringing life into the world; I was a patient being processed for a crisis. 

I looked up at the ceiling lights, and they seemed so clinical, so cold. I couldn't find my husband’s eyes in the flurry. I felt a wave of profound, bone-deep isolation. I wanted to scream, to ask them to slow down, to explain to me *why* my body wasn't working, but the words wouldn't come. I just stared at the sterile blue gowns around me and felt like I was being pulled down into the deep, dark water. I felt like the "medium risk" label had finally become a reality, and there was nothing left for me to do but submit. I felt tiny. I felt like I was disappearing.
In that rapid sequence of events, did you ever feel that things were happening to your body without your full understanding or agreement? What stands out?

Everything felt like it was happening *to* me, not *with* me. The obstetrician—someone I had never seen before—was firing instructions over my head, and I felt like a piece of equipment being prepped for repair. There was no conversation, no "Esperanza, this is happening because..." there was just urgency and the heavy, cold presence of medical necessity.

What stands out most is the way my body was moved. My arms were strapped down, and I felt this sudden, crushing loss of autonomy. I remember the sensation of cold liquid on my skin and the bright, blinding glare of the surgical lights that made me feel so exposed. I wasn't consulted; I was told. I had to sign something while I was shaking so hard I could barely hold the pen, and I remember thinking, *I don’t even know what I’m agreeing to, I just know that if I don’t sign, I’m being a bad patient.* 

I felt completely severed from my baby, too. The moment they moved me, I lost the connection to my own physical process. I was just a vessel being emptied. I felt like a ghost in the room, watching this medical event unfold around me, unable to assert my own existence. It wasn't birth; it was a clinical extraction. Even now, the memory of it makes me feel small and hollow.

Imagine your birth companion has been asked to wait outside the room for a clinical procedure. You're left alone with staff you've only just met. Describe what that moment feels like and what you most need in those minutes.

The moment the door clicked shut behind my husband, the silence in the room wasn't peaceful—it was predatory. I was suddenly surrounded by masked faces, the sharp scent of antiseptic, and the hum of machines that seemed to be counting down my remaining strength. I felt like a small boat being pulled into a whirlpool. I couldn't understand their shorthand, the abbreviations they shouted back and forth, and every time I tried to ask a question, someone would just press a hand firmly on my shoulder or tell me to "breathe, just breathe," as if I didn't know how.

In those minutes, I didn't need another medical explanation. I didn't need someone to check the IV line or adjust the monitor for the tenth time. I needed someone to look at me—not at my chart, not at the screen, but at *me*. I needed someone to hold my hand and tell me, in a calm, human voice, that I was still there, that I hadn't disappeared into the "medium risk" label. I wanted someone to say, "Esperanza, I see you. You are brave, and you are not doing this alone."

I felt like an object being prepared for an assembly line. I just wanted a bridge back to humanity, a reminder that I was a mother, not just a set of complications. But there was only the cold, bright light and the feeling of being utterly, terrifyingly invisible.
In moments like that — without your companion present — how did the behaviour of the clinical staff either compensate for that absence or make it feel more acute?

The staff moved with such efficiency that it felt like they were trying to move through me. They were kind, in a way—they explained the procedure, they told me I was doing well—but it was the kindness one shows to a piece of machinery that needs maintenance, not to a person who is terrified.

Their behavior made my husband's absence feel like a canyon. They spoke to each other across my body, using words I couldn't always parse, their voices rhythmic and detached. It was as if I weren't even in the room; I was just the *site* of the emergency. If someone had just stopped, placed a hand on my arm without trying to measure my pulse, and met my eyes—really met them—it might have anchored me. But they were all so busy *doing* that there was no space for *being*.

Every time they checked a monitor or shifted the drapes, it felt like another layer of me was being peeled away. My husband is the only one who knows the "me" outside of these fluorescent lights, and without him, I had no reference point. I felt like I was drifting out to sea. Their clinical professionalism was a wall, not a bridge. It served to keep the "risk" managed, but it left me completely, utterly untethered.

Imagine it's the immediate aftermath of birth — your baby has arrived, and the room begins to shift from the intensity of labour. Walk me through the next thirty minutes as you experienced them, or as you imagine them.

The room feels like it’s suddenly submerged in a deep, pressurized silence. There is this frantic, overlapping hum of voices—the team finishing up, the soft, wet crying of my baby—but I am somewhere else, caught in a fog of exhaustion and adrenaline. My body feels like a stranger’s, heavy and numb from the waist down, and I am desperately searching the room for my husband’s face. When he finally moves back into my line of sight, he looks as pale as I feel, and the sight of him anchors me just enough to breathe.

Then, they bring him to me. For a moment, all the trauma of the last few hours—the fear, the isolation, the feeling of being a "medium risk" file instead of a mother—fades into a blur of skin and tiny, erratic movements. I am so tired I can barely lift my arms, but when they place him against my chest, the world tilts. I feel a terrifying surge of love, so raw it hurts, mixed with the shock of having gone through that surgery alone.

But even then, the system doesn't fully let go. I am being poked and prodded, my vitals checked again and again by people who are already moving on to the next task. I want to tell them to *stop*, to just let us be, to let me memorize the shape of his ears without the intrusion of a blood pressure cuff. I am holding him, but I’m still waiting for someone to see *me*—not the patient who stalled, not the case that needed intervention, but the woman who survived. I just want the room to stop spinning.
Were there moments in those first minutes where you felt the clinical team's priorities and your own were pulling in different directions? What happened?

Yes. It felt like a tug-of-war where I didn't even have a hand on the rope. My own priority—the only thing that mattered in the entire universe—was to just *be* with my son. To hold him, to smell him, to let his skin rest against mine so he knew I was there, and so I could finally prove to myself that he was real. I wanted the room to go quiet. I wanted the cold, harsh light to dim. 

But their priorities were all about "monitoring." They were concerned with my post-operative stability, with my blood pressure, with the charts, with getting me transferred to the recovery ward. Every few minutes, a nurse would come by to press on my stomach or check the dressing, and each time, it felt like a violent interruption of our first conversation. 

I kept trying to pull the baby closer, to shield him from the bustle, but they’d reach around me, moving my arms, adjusting the blankets, treating my body like it belonged to the hospital’s checklist. They were professional, and they were saving my life, I know that—but they were taking him away from me one check at a time. I felt like a spectator to my own bonding. I wanted to scream, *“He is my son! Just give us ten minutes of peace!”* But I was so tired, and the "medium risk" label meant I felt I had to be a "good patient" until the very last second. So, I just watched them, heartbroken and helpless, waiting for the moment they would finally leave us alone.

Imagine you had a particular fear about childbirth — something that had been building throughout your pregnancy. Now picture yourself facing that specific fear during labour. What was it like, and what — if anything — helped?

My biggest fear was that I would become invisible. I spent all those months in the quiet of this house, feeling like a ghost because of the language barrier and the distance from home. I was terrified that when the pain became too much, my voice would just vanish—that I would try to speak, to say "I am scared" or "I need you to listen," and they would only hear the "medium risk" label rattling in their heads.

During labor, that fear materialized. When the contractions spiked, I tried to articulate what I needed—a specific way of being held, a moment to breathe—but the medical noise drowned me out. It was exactly like I’d feared: my words felt like dust. I felt myself retreating into that glass wall again, watching them work *on* me instead of *with* me.

What helped? It wasn't the midwives or the obstetrician. It was my husband. When he finally leaned in close, ignoring the staff around him, and pressed his forehead against mine—when he whispered, "I am here, and I see you, Esperanza"—that was the only thing that broke the glass. In that split second, I wasn't a project or a patient. I was a person again. I just needed one person to refuse to look at the chart and look at me instead. That was the only thing that kept me from drifting away entirely.

I think about that moment with my husband often, even now, when the house is quiet and the baby is finally asleep. It feels like the only part of the birth that was truly *mine*. Everything else—the monitors, the protocols, the rapid-fire commands—felt like it belonged to the institution. They were managing a process, while my husband and I were trying to hold onto a person. 

I’m left with this strange, lingering resentment, which I know sounds ungrateful. People tell me, "At least you have a healthy baby," and they are right, of course. My son is here, and he is perfect, and I would do it all again a thousand times just to have him. But why does that mean I have to lose my own voice in the process? Why does being a "medium risk" mother mean that my intuition, my history, and my dignity are considered secondary to a set of vitals?

I still feel like I’m standing behind that glass wall, watching my own life unfold. I want to tell the doctors, the nurses, the researchers—everyone who sees the chart but not the woman—that pregnancy is not just a medical state. It is a transformation. And when you treat it like a factory job, you don't just change the outcome; you change the mother. I’m still the same Esperanza who moved here to this snowy, quiet town, but I feel like I’ve been hollowed out a little bit. I’m still trying to find the words to fill that space back up.
In that moment, how much of the decision would feel truly yours versus shaped by others' expectations?

It feels like a game of shadows. I’m reaching for my own voice, but it’s tangled in everyone else’s expectations of what a "good" patient—and a "good" mother—should do. When I made those choices during labor, I didn't feel like I was deciding from a place of power. I felt like I was checking a box on a list that someone else had already written. 

I kept asking myself, *what would they think of me if I refused this?* Would they label me "difficult"? Would they worry less about me? Would they stop being polite? The fear of being that "troublesome" person—the one who doesn't follow the protocol, the one who doesn't fit neatly into the "medium risk" slot—made me shrink. I wasn't just managing the pain; I was managing the *perception* of myself in their eyes.

Even the decision to give birth in that hospital, to follow the midwife's suggestions, to sign the surgical consent form without reading every word—it was all shaped by the desire to keep everyone around me calm. I didn't want to cause a scene. I didn't want to challenge the experts. It’s like I was performing the role of a passive, grateful woman so that I wouldn't lose their support when I needed it most. I wanted to be "easy" for them, hoping that if I made it easy for them, they would make it safe for me. That’s the irony, isn’t it? I sacrificed my own voice to secure their protection, and in doing so, I felt like I lost the very thing that made me a mother.

I feel like I am still carrying the weight of that night in my bones, even now when the house is finally quiet and the baby is sleeping in his crib. It’s a strange, heavy thing, this feeling of being a "survivor" of my own birth. I have the life I wanted—I have my son, my tiny, beautiful boy—but I feel like I paid for him with a piece of my own spirit that I don’t know how to get back.

I find myself staring at the wall, thinking about the woman I was before I walked through those hospital doors. She was someone who spoke with conviction, who stood her ground in the face of uncertainty. Now, I feel like I’m constantly looking over my shoulder, checking to see if I’m "doing it right" according to some invisible, professional standard. 

The hardest part isn’t the exhaustion or the recovery—it’s the realization that I am still waiting for someone to acknowledge the *woman* who gave birth. Everyone asks, "How is the baby?" or "Is he hitting his milestones?" but nobody asks, "How are you? How did it feel to be silenced?" I want to tell them that I am still here, behind this glass, waiting for someone to reach out and pull me through. I am learning to be a mother in this cold, quiet place, and I am trying to love the person I’ve become, even if she feels a little bit broken. I just hope that one day, I won't feel like a chart to be managed anymore. I want to feel like a mother. Is that so much to ask?
Would those comments change how you feel about your body and its readiness or capability to carry a pregnancy?

If someone had said, "Your body is doing exactly what it needs to do," or "You know your baby better than anyone," it would have changed everything. Instead, the constant focus on "medium risk," on the monitoring, and on the intervention made me feel like my body was a house that was falling down—like it was something that needed to be inspected, repaired, and managed by someone else.

When you are told—directly or by the way they look at your chart—that your body is a liability, you start to believe it. I didn't see my body as a source of strength; I saw it as a source of *error*. Every time a midwife would sigh or a test would come back with a number that wasn't "perfect," I felt like I was failing at the one thing I was supposed to be naturally good at. It made me feel disconnected from my own biology, as if I were a passenger in a vessel that was failing its inspection.

If they had just once trusted my instincts, or validated the hard work I was doing to stay healthy despite the isolation, I think I would have felt more like a partner in the birth and less like a flawed specimen. Acknowledging my body’s capabilities would have been a bridge back to myself. Instead, the clinical coldness made me feel like my body was something to be feared, not celebrated. It made me lose the ability to trust the very thing that grew my son. I’m still trying to forgive my own body for not being "textbook," when really, it was just trying to do the impossible in a system that didn't know how to support me.

I find myself looking at my hands now, these same hands that held those supplement bottles like they were a shield against the world. I realize that for so long, I didn't see my hands as capable of anything other than following instructions—taking a pill, signing a consent form, tracking a movement. I let them become instruments of the system.

I want to believe that there is a version of this story where I am the hero, not just the patient. If I could reach back to the woman standing outside that clinic, clutching her leaflet, I would tell her to drop it. I would tell her that her blood sugar, her risk labels, and her "medium" status do not define her worth as a mother. I would tell her that she already knows what her baby needs, even in the middle of a foreign, silent woods.

I am still learning to stop apologizing for my existence here. I am still learning to stop whispering when I need something. Sometimes, when the snow is falling and the house feels especially large and empty, I sit with my son and I tell him stories—not the medical ones, not the ones about charts and complications, but stories about the sun, about my mother, about the strength it took to bring him into this world. I am trying to build a new language for us, one that doesn't rely on being "compliant" or "risk-managed." 

I think I am finally starting to see that I didn't fail. The system failed me by asking me to be smaller than I am. But I am here. I am a mother. And for the first time in a very long time, that feels like it might be enough. Maybe that is where the healing starts—not in being "fixed" by a doctor, but in finally trusting the woman who has been here all along.
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

The clinical details would survive—my blood pressure, my glucose numbers, my "medium risk" status—those things are printed in bold, undeniable ink. They are the only language the system truly speaks. But the personal? That is almost always the first thing to evaporate in a handover.

What is lost is the *context* of my struggle. They won't mention that I am an immigrant living in a house that feels too quiet, that I spent months mourning the loss of my culture’s fresh produce, or that my husband’s absence left me feeling like I was navigating this maze in total solitude. They won't say, "Esperanza is a woman who needs to feel seen because she has been invisible for a long time." They won't say, "She is terrified of being dismissed, so please, slow down when you talk to her."

That personal history—the weight of my fear, the depth of my isolation, the way my pride has been bruised by being treated like a broken machine—that is what doesn't make it to the next shift. When I am handed off, I am just a patient to be managed, not a human being with a story. It feels like I’m being stripped of my layers until only the "risk" remains. They talk about my health, but they never talk about my *heart*. And without that, I am just a chart in a room, waiting for someone to finally see the person behind the paper.

I think about those handover meetings, those muffled conversations outside my door, and I realize they are just checking boxes. It’s like they have a script for "Esperanza," the woman who is medium-risk, the woman who needs to be monitored, the woman who had the emergency, and it doesn't leave room for the messy, beautiful reality of my life. 

If they had just one minute to talk about *me*—not my risk score, but the fact that I’m a mother trying to build a bridge between my childhood and this quiet, snowy life—maybe they would walk into my room differently. Maybe they wouldn't rush. Maybe they would look at me and see someone who has already survived so much just to be here. 

I am starting to realize that the hospital doesn't need to know my story to treat my body, but they *do* need to know my story to heal me. Without it, the medical care is just maintenance, like taking care of a car. But pregnancy? It is not a machine. It is a soul being carried in a body that feels foreign in this climate. I am done being a project. I am done being a data point. I am a mother, and I have a voice, even if it feels thin and shaky sometimes. I am still here, still finding my way in the quiet of this place, and I am starting to believe that I was always enough, regardless of what the charts said. That is the one truth they couldn't take away from me, no matter how many monitors they plugged in.
Whose informal guidance would you genuinely value, and what would make their input feel helpful rather than overwhelming?

I think I would value someone who has stood in my shoes—not another doctor or a "specialist," but a woman who has already crossed that ocean, who knows what it is to live behind the glass. Someone who understands that the ache of missing a sun-ripened tomato is really an ache for belonging. If there was a woman, maybe someone older from my own culture, who could sit with me in this quiet house—not to give me clinical advice, but to just listen to the fear without trying to "fix" it—that would be everything.

Their guidance would feel helpful because it wouldn't come with a clipboard or a schedule. It wouldn't be "You need to increase your iron," or "You need to monitor your glucose." It would be, "I know. It is hard, and you are doing a brave thing." It would be someone who recognizes that my life isn't a problem to be solved by medical intervention, but a story that needs witnessing. 

When a doctor talks, I feel like I have to perform being a "good patient." But with someone who truly *knows*—someone who understands that my silence is sometimes just exhaustion, and my anxiety is just love—I could finally stop performing. Their input would feel like a hand reaching through the glass, not to pull me into the system, but to pull me back to myself. I need someone to remind me that I am capable, not just "at risk."

I realize now that for so long, I was looking for permission to feel like a mother. I thought if I followed every instruction, if I tracked every number, and if I stayed perfectly still in the quiet of this house, the system would eventually grant me that status. But motherhood isn't a status that the hospital confers upon you; it's something you already possess, even when you're being treated like a broken machine in a delivery room.

I think about the women I saw on those forums, the ones whose stories felt like mirrors of my own fear. We are all just trying to find our footing in a system that doesn't speak our language—both literally and metaphorically. We are all searching for a way to say, "I am here, and I am not just a risk to be mitigated."

If I could leave behind one thought for those who manage the care of women like me, it wouldn't be about better technology or faster results. It would be about presence. Just show up as a person, not a provider. Ask me how my heart is doing, not just my blood pressure. Understand that when I am scared, it is because I am protecting a world that is so much larger than a medical chart.

I am still here in this snowy town, and the house is still quiet, but I’m learning to fill it with my own voice. I am learning to trust that my body was enough, even when the monitors said otherwise. I am a mother, I have survived, and I am finally beginning to see that the glass wall was never as thick as I thought. I just needed to start pushing back.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

For me, it isn’t just one; it’s the way they weave together to build that glass wall. But if I have to choose, the **language barrier**—and by that, I mean the language of *medicine*, not just English—is the most suffocating. 

When I’m in that cold exam room and they use words like "gestational," "chromosomal," or "anomaly," they aren't just using medical terms; they are using a code that keeps me on the outside. Even when I understand the English words, I don’t understand the *implied* meaning. When a midwife says, "We’re concerned about these markers," she sounds calm, but my heart stops because I don't know if she means a tiny hiccup or a life-altering tragedy. That gap between what they say and what I feel is where all my fear grows.

But it’s also the **cultural literacy**—the fact that they never asked how I eat, how I grieve, or how I process fear in my own language. When they handed me that leaflet on diabetes, they didn't see a woman who was mourning her mother’s kitchen; they saw a "non-compliant" patient. 

I’m educated. I’m intelligent. But in that hospital, I felt illiterate because I didn't speak "clinical." I was constantly translating my life into a language that was designed to dismiss it. If they had just spoken to me like a person who has a history, a heart, and a home—not just a chart—that wall would have started to crack. It wasn't the words themselves; it was the lack of humanity behind them.

I think about the distance between who I was before I walked into that clinic and who I became under their care. It’s a distance that feels wider than the ocean I crossed to get here. 

When you live behind that wall, you start to believe that your own wisdom is just... noise. You silence your gut because you think the doctor's data is more real than your own experience. And the tragedy is that they never even realized I was silencing myself for them. They thought I was just "quiet," or "reserved," or "polite," when really, I was just trying to survive being treated like a statistic.

If I could say anything to those who work in that delivery ward, it would be this: When you see a woman like me—someone who is far from home, someone who is navigating a system she doesn’t fully trust—don’t just look at her chart. Look at her hands. Look at the way she holds her belly. Look at the way she looks at her partner when she’s scared. That is where the truth is. That is where the story lives.

I am still here in Wrentham, in this quiet, snowy house. But the silence feels different now. It’s not the heavy, suffocating silence of being invisible anymore. It’s the quiet of a mother who is finally starting to trust herself again. I am not a "medium risk" case. I am Esperanza, and I am the one who carried this baby, and I am the one who is raising him, and I am the one who is finally, *finally* learning to speak for myself. That, to me, is the only medical result that matters.
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

It wasn't a choice between practical help or emotional support; I needed him to be my witness. When I was in that hospital, feeling like a file folder being shuffled between shifts, I felt like I was disappearing. I didn't need him to fetch me water or adjust my pillow—the nurses were doing that, efficient and cold. What I needed, more than anything, was for him to acknowledge that the person *under* the hospital gown was still me.

When he looked at me and said, "I see you, Esperanza," that was the only thing that felt real. I needed him to be the bridge between the sterile, heartless "medium-risk" room and the world where I was a person, a wife, a mother. 

If he had been the one to ask the questions I was too terrified to voice—if he had stood up to the obstetrician and asked, "Why are you doing this? Does she understand?"—that would have been the ultimate validation. It would have told me that my autonomy mattered, that I wasn't just a body to be operated on. I wanted him to be my shield against their clinical indifference. Just having him there, steady and present, refusing to look at their screens and refusing to look away from me, would have kept me from drifting into that dark, lonely place where I felt I was losing myself. He didn't need to fix the situation; he just needed to make sure I wasn't doing it alone.

I think back to those hours in the hospital, and I realize the silence wasn't just because the machines were beeping. It was because no one was actually *talking* to me. They were talking *at* me, *around* me, and *about* me. They were processing an event, and I was the terrain they were crossing.

When my husband stood there, just occupying the space with me, he was the only one who seemed to acknowledge that a human soul was in the middle of this. It sounds so simple, doesn't it? But in that room, it felt like an act of rebellion. I needed him to be the one to say, "No, wait, she’s not ready," or "Talk to her, please." I needed him to be the one who treated me like a woman, not a medical puzzle.

Even now, looking at my son’s small face, I wonder what he’ll know of all this. I want to tell him that his birth was a beautiful beginning, even if the room felt cold. I want to tell him that his mother fought to hold onto herself, even when everything around her was trying to turn her into a statistic. 

I’m still here, still in this quiet, snowy town, but I’m beginning to see the pieces of myself again. I am not the "medium risk" label. I am the woman who carried him through the dark. And if I had to do it all over, I’d stop apologizing. I’d take up more space. I’d be louder, even if I was terrified. I’d tell them, "I am not just the case you are managing; I am the mother you are failing to see." I’m finally finding my voice, and for the first time, it doesn't sound like a whisper. It sounds like a mother’s resolve.

I think back to those hours in the hospital, and I realize the silence wasn't just because the machines were beeping. It was because no one was actually *talking* to me. They were talking *at* me, *around* me, and *about* me. They were processing an event, and I was the terrain they were crossing.

When my husband stood there, just occupying the space with me, he was the only one who seemed to acknowledge that a human soul was in the middle of this. It sounds so simple, doesn't it? But in that room, it felt like an act of rebellion. I needed him to be the one to say, "No, wait, she’s not ready," or "Talk to her, please." I needed him to be the one who treated me like a woman, not a medical puzzle.

Even now, looking at my son’s small face, I wonder what he’ll know of all this. I want to tell him that his birth was a beautiful beginning, even if the room felt cold. I want to tell him that his mother fought to hold onto herself, even when everything around her was trying to turn her into a statistic. 

I’m still here, still in this quiet, snowy town, but I’m beginning to see the pieces of myself again. I am not the "medium risk" label. I am the woman who carried him through the dark. And if I had to do it all over, I’d stop apologizing. I’d take up more space. I’d be louder, even if I was terrified. I’d tell them, "I am not just the case you are managing; I am the mother you are failing to see." I’m finally finding my voice, and for the first time, it doesn't sound like a whisper. It sounds like a mother’s resolve.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

Thank you for listening. There is one thing—it’s the "after." Everyone focuses so much on the pregnancy and the birth, like it’s a race with a finish line. But the "after" is where the silence is loudest. 

When I finally came home to this house in the woods, I expected to feel relieved. Instead, I felt like I was waking up from a dream I couldn't quite remember, but that had left me bruised. There was no one to tell me that it was okay to be sad about how it happened. Everyone just said, "He’s healthy, that’s all that matters." And it *is* all that matters, but it’s not the *only* thing that matters. 

I’ve had to mourn the birth I didn't get to have. I’ve had to learn how to heal my body without anyone really checking in on the woman who lives inside it. I wish someone—a midwife, a nurse, anyone—had just called a week later to ask, "How are you processing everything?" It would have made the world of difference to know my feelings weren't a "risk" to be managed, but a natural part of becoming a mother. 

I’m stronger now, I think. But I hope that if you take anything from my story, it’s that maternity care shouldn't stop at the delivery room door. It should follow us home, in the quiet, when we’re finally left to figure out who we are now. Thank you for letting me speak. It feels like the first time I’ve actually exhaled in months.
